name: React deep dive (Artec)
description: The Artec frontend prep doc in depth: rendering model, re-render optimization, state strategy, SSR vs CSR, app structure, legacy migration, quality gates, API contracts, and AI-assisted development.

topics.name: React rendering model
topics.description: Render vs commit, reconciliation, keys, and what actually triggers a re-render.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

How React actually gets your components onto the screen: it re-runs component functions, compares the result with the previous one, and applies only the real differences to the page.

## What problem does it solve?

Updating the real DOM is slow, and doing it by hand is error-prone. React lets you describe what the UI should look like for the current data, and it figures out the minimal changes itself.

## How it works

Rendering happens in two phases:
- Render phase: React calls your component functions and builds a lightweight description of the UI (the "virtual DOM"), then compares it with the previous description. This comparison is called reconciliation. The render phase must be PURE — no side effects — because React may run it several times or throw the result away.
- Commit phase: React applies only the actual differences to the real DOM, then runs your effects.

What triggers a re-render:
- The component's own state changes (a useState setter, a useReducer dispatch).
- The PARENT re-renders — children re-run by default, even if their props did not change.
- A context value the component reads changes.

What does NOT trigger one: mutating an object or array in place. React compares by reference — if you push into the same array, the reference is unchanged and React sees nothing. This is the classic "why doesn't my UI update" bug, and the reason state updates must create new objects.

Important distinction: a re-render is NOT a DOM update. Component functions re-running is cheap; the browser DOM only changes where reconciliation found a real difference.

For lists, the key prop tells React which item is which across renders. Using the array index as key breaks when the list reorders — state and focus stick to the position instead of the item.

## Common mistakes

- Mutating state in place and wondering why nothing updates (reference equality)
- Using array indexes as keys on a reorderable list
- Treating every re-render as a performance problem — the DOM only changes where the diff says so

## Key terms

- Virtual DOM — React's in-memory description of the UI
- Reconciliation — diffing the new description against the old
- Render vs commit — compute the changes vs apply them
- key — stable identity for list items (never the index for dynamic lists)
- Reference equality — why mutation is invisible to React

topics.name: SSR vs CSR
topics.description: Server-side vs client-side rendering, hydration, and when a framework like Next.js earns its complexity.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Two strategies for producing a web page. CSR (client-side rendering): the server sends an almost empty HTML file plus JavaScript, and the browser builds the page. SSR (server-side rendering): the server builds the finished HTML and sends that. Frameworks like Next.js exist mainly to make SSR practical with React.

## What problem does it solve?

With CSR, the user stares at a blank page until the JavaScript downloads and runs, and search engines may see nothing to index. SSR fixes both: real HTML arrives immediately.

## How it works

The decision comes down to who visits the page:
- Public, content-heavy pages (shops, blogs, marketing) → SSR or SSG (static generation: the HTML is built once at build time). Crawlers get indexable HTML; users see content fast.
- Apps behind a login (dashboards, admin tools) → CSR is fine. There is nothing to index, and SSR would add servers, complexity and cost for no visible benefit.

The catch in SSR is hydration: the server sends finished HTML, then React loads in the browser, builds the same component tree, and ATTACHES event handlers to the existing HTML. Until hydration finishes, the page LOOKS interactive but does not respond. And if the server's HTML differs from what the client would render (dates, random values, browser-only APIs used during render), you get hydration mismatch errors.

React vs Next.js in one sentence: React is a UI library (components); Next.js is a framework on top of it that adds routing, SSR/SSG, image optimization and production structure. The honest question before adopting it: "is anything here public and SEO- or first-paint-critical?" If not, plain React as a SPA is the simpler system.

## Common mistakes

- Recommending Next.js for an internal dashboard behind a login "for SEO"
- Rendering dates/random values during SSR → hydration mismatch
- Assuming a server-rendered page is interactive before hydration finishes

## Key terms

- CSR / SSR / SSG — browser-built, server-built per request, server-built at build time
- Hydration — attaching React behavior to server-sent HTML
- Hydration mismatch — server and client HTML disagree
- SEO / first paint — the two reasons SSR earns its complexity

topics.name: Re-render Optimization & Memoization
topics.description: Measure first; React.memo/useMemo/useCallback, stable references, and rendering huge lists.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

React's three memoization tools — React.memo, useMemo, useCallback — which let components and computations skip re-running when their inputs have not changed.

## What problem does it solve?

When a parent re-renders, all its children re-run by default. Usually that is cheap and fine. But when a child subtree is big or a computation is heavy, re-running it on every keystroke makes the UI lag.

## How it works

First, the senior rule: MEASURE before optimizing. The usual real causes of slowness are state living too high in the tree (so everything under it re-renders), props whose references change every render, giant lists, and oversized context values. Fix the structure first: move state closer to where it is used, split big components.

Then the tools:
- React.memo(Child) — skip re-rendering the child if its props are unchanged.
- useMemo(fn, deps) — cache a computed VALUE between renders.
- useCallback(fn, deps) — cache a FUNCTION reference between renders.

The trap that connects them: comparison is BY REFERENCE. If the parent creates a fresh object or an inline arrow function on every render and passes it as a prop, the memoized child sees a "new" prop every time and re-renders anyway — the memo is defeated. That is exactly what useMemo/useCallback are for: keeping those references stable.

The huge-list question ("render a million rows"): don't. Paginate on the backend, and on the client use virtualization/windowing (react-window) — only the ~20 visible rows exist in the DOM, swapped as you scroll.

Related JS patterns: debounce (run after the input goes quiet — search fields) and throttle (run at most every N ms — scroll handlers).

## Common mistakes

- Memoizing everything preemptively instead of measuring first
- React.memo on the child while passing a fresh inline object/function from the parent
- Rendering huge lists fully instead of virtualizing (react-window)

## Key terms

- React.memo / useMemo / useCallback — skip child / cache value / cache function
- Stable reference — what memoization needs to work
- Virtualization — render only the visible rows of a huge list
- Debounce / throttle — taming high-frequency events

topics.name: Choosing a State Strategy
topics.description: Local vs shared vs server vs navigation state; prop drilling, Context+useReducer, and when Redux earns its place.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A decision guide for WHERE state should live in a React app — because "put everything in one global store" and "pass everything through props" are both wrong.

## What problem does it solve?

Different kinds of state have different needs. Treating them all the same leads either to a bloated global store or to props threaded through ten components.

## How it works

Sort the state by type first:
- Local UI state (is the modal open, which tab is selected) → useState/useReducer in the component.
- Simple shared state (theme, language, current user) → React Context.
- Complex app state (many modules reacting to the same events) → Redux or similar.
- Server data (API results) → React Query / SWR — this is a CACHE, not app state; it needs refetching and invalidation, which a store does not give you.
- Navigation state (filters, page number) → the URL, so it survives refresh and can be shared.

Prop drilling: passing state down one or two levels is completely fine. It becomes a problem when unrelated middle components forward props they never use — then reach for Context or composition.

Context + useReducer works as a lightweight Redux: Context delivers the value to the subtree, useReducer gives predictable updates. Its limits: no devtools/time-travel, no middleware, and every consumer re-renders when the value changes — so split contexts by domain (AuthContext, ThemeContext) instead of one giant one.

When Redux genuinely earns its place: multiple independent modules reacting to the same app-level events, predictable/debuggable updates, and a selector layer that hides the store's shape from components. A senior touch: expose domain actions (login(), updateFilter()) rather than raw setters.

## Common mistakes

- Server data in a global store instead of a query cache (React Query/SWR)
- One giant context whose every change re-renders every consumer
- Filters/pagination in component state — put them in the URL so refresh and sharing work

## Key terms

- State types — local / shared / server / navigation, each with its home
- Prop drilling — props passing through components that don't use them
- Server state — API data belongs in a query cache, not a store
- Selector — a function hiding the store's shape from components

topics.name: App Structure & Reusable Components
topics.description: Feature-based boundaries, when a component deserves to be reusable, and working from Figma designs.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

How to organize a large React codebase — and when to make a component reusable versus keeping it specific.

## What problem does it solve?

Organizing by file type (all components in one folder, all hooks in another) or purely by route stops working as an app grows: related code ends up scattered, and every feature touches five folders.

## How it works

Organize by FEATURE: everything belonging to "payments" — its components, hooks, API calls, state — lives together in features/payments. Routes become thin entry points that compose features. A typical layout:

- app/ — providers, store, routing setup
- pages/ — route entry points
- features/ — user, payments, dashboard… (each self-contained)
- shared/ — genuinely generic UI, utils, hooks, types

Only promote code to shared/ when it is actually generic — not "might be reused someday".

Reusable components: the senior stance is restraint. Make a component reusable when the same pattern actually appears in multiple places, not preemptively. A good reusable component has a small clear API, sensible defaults, and no product-specific logic. Warning sign: a dozen configuration props usually means it should be split.

Working from Figma designs, the value is spotting what is MISSING: loading/error/empty/disabled states, responsive behavior, long translated texts, hover/focus/keyboard interactions. Ask the designer early — not at sprint end.

## Common mistakes

- Folders by file type (components/, hooks/) that scatter every feature
- Generalizing a component before a second real use exists
- Building only the happy path from a Figma design — the missing states are the work

## Key terms

- Feature-based structure — code grouped by domain, not by file type
- Entry point — a route that composes features, holding no logic itself
- Premature abstraction — generalizing before a real second use exists
- Missing states — loading/error/empty/disabled that designs often omit

topics.name: Legacy Frontend Migration
topics.description: jQuery/Rails templates to React without a big bang, and modernizing an outdated React codebase.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The strategy for moving an old frontend (jQuery, server templates, or outdated React) to modern React without stopping feature work or breaking the product.

## What problem does it solve?

The tempting "big rewrite" — freeze everything, rebuild from scratch — historically fails: it takes twice as long as planned, the old system keeps changing underneath it, and the business gets nothing until the very end.

## How it works

Migrate incrementally (the "strangler" approach):
- Understand the existing behavior first; add tests around the critical flows BEFORE touching them — they are your safety net.
- Find isolated areas where React can be introduced safely; replace them one at a time in small, reviewable steps.
- Keep behavior stable at each step; build NEW functionality in React so the legacy surface shrinks naturally.

Choose what to refactor by RISK and VALUE, not by taste: prioritize code that changes often, blocks new work, or is genuinely fragile. Leave stable legacy code alone — rewriting working code "because it's old" is cost without benefit.

For an outdated React codebase specifically: audit dependencies first (React, router, build tools, test libs); hunt deprecated APIs (legacy lifecycles, ReactDOM.render → createRoot); update in small steps — dependencies, then build, then tests green, then pattern cleanup. Do NOT mass-rewrite class components to hooks; convert only the ones you actively work on.

## Common mistakes

- The big-bang rewrite — twice the estimate, business value only at the end
- Refactoring stable legacy code for style points
- Mass-converting class components to hooks with no concrete reason
- Touching risky flows without tests around them first

## Key terms

- Big-bang rewrite — the anti-pattern: all-at-once replacement
- Strangler pattern — new code gradually surrounds and replaces old
- Safety net — tests around critical flows before changing them
- createRoot — the React 18+ entry point replacing ReactDOM.render

topics.name: Code Quality, Testing & Release Safety
topics.description: The quality tool layers, regression and visual regression testing, and feature flags.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The layers of tooling and process that catch mistakes before users see them — from type checking to feature flags.

## What problem does it solve?

Humans make mistakes at a steady rate. Each layer catches a class of mistakes as early — and therefore as cheaply — as possible.

## How it works

The layers, from fastest feedback to slowest:
- TypeScript — type errors while you type.
- ESLint — suspicious patterns, broken hooks rules, unused code.
- Prettier — formatting handled by a tool, so reviews discuss substance.
- Unit/component tests — Jest/Vitest + React Testing Library for logic and component behavior.
- E2E tests — Playwright/Cypress driving a real browser through the few critical user flows (login, purchase). Expensive, so reserve them for what must never break.
- Visual regression — screenshot comparison (Chromatic/Loki) catching unintended look changes.
- CI gates — all of the above must pass before merge.
- Code review and dependency audits (npm audit, Dependabot) — the human and supply-chain layers.

Regression testing just means verifying that new changes did not break what already worked — most of the layers above are automated regression tests.

Release safety: feature flags separate DEPLOYING code from ENABLING it. Ship the code dark, turn it on for staff → 5% of users → everyone; if metrics go bad, turn it OFF with a switch instead of an emergency rollback deploy. The cost to manage: old flags pile up as debt — cleanup must be routine.

## Common mistakes

- E2E tests for everything — slow, flaky, expensive; reserve them for critical flows
- Feature flags that never get cleaned up (flag debt)
- A green pipeline with no visual regression — the UI can still silently break

## Key terms

- Quality gates — checks that must pass before merge/release
- E2E test — a scripted browser walking a real user flow
- Visual regression — screenshot diffs against approved baselines
- Feature flag — deploy ≠ enable; gradual rollout + kill switch
- Flag debt — stale flags that must be cleaned up

topics.name: AI-Assisted Development
topics.description: Using AI as an accelerator while keeping engineering responsibility.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Using AI tools (Copilot, Claude, ChatGPT) as part of the daily development workflow — and the professional judgment about where their output can be trusted.

## What problem does it solve?

A large share of coding time goes to boilerplate, repetitive refactors, test scaffolding and documentation. AI accelerates exactly those — freeing time for the parts that need human judgment.

## How it works

Where it genuinely helps: generating boilerplate, suggesting refactorings, drafting tests and docs, and quickly exploring "what are three ways to do X".

What does not change: generated code is NOT automatically correct. It still gets reviewed for edge cases, types, security, performance, accessibility and maintainability — the same bar as human code. The engineer remains responsible for what ships.

The guardrails that make AI-generated code safe are the ordinary ones, which become MORE important, not less: strong tests, type checking, code review, and clear architectural ownership.

The failure mode to name: shipping code you cannot explain. Code you don't understand, you cannot debug, secure, or evolve — no matter who or what wrote it. If asked "we barely write code by hand anymore", the mature response is curiosity about their workflow plus the same principle: generation speeds up implementation; it does not remove the need for engineering judgment.

## Common mistakes

- Shipping generated code nobody on the team can explain
- Treating AI output as reviewed-by-default instead of held to the same bar
- Letting tests and types erode exactly when generation makes them more critical

## Key terms

- Accelerator, not replacement — the healthy framing
- Review bar — generated code meets the same standard as human code
- Guardrails — tests, types, review, ownership
- Explainability — never ship what you cannot explain

questions.title: What triggers a React re-render
questions.prompt: Walk me through React's rendering model: what triggers a re-render, and what happens between a state change and the DOM update?
questions.answerStructureHint: State/prop/context change → render phase (reconciliation, diffing) → commit phase (DOM mutations); children re-render with parents by default.

expectedPoints.label: Knows what triggers renders
expectedPoints.description: setState, parent re-render, and context value change — not prop mutation by itself.

expectedPoints.label: Render vs commit, reconciliation
expectedPoints.description: Render phase builds the new virtual tree and diffs it; commit applies minimal DOM changes; keys drive list diffing.

expectedPoints.label: Render must be pure
expectedPoints.description: Render can run many times without side effects; effects run after commit.

followUps.prompt: Why is using an array index as a key a problem in a reorderable list?

questions.title: State management without Redux
questions.prompt: When do you actually need a state management library in React? How far do you get without one?
questions.answerStructureHint: Local state first, lift when shared, context for stable app-wide values, server-cache libraries for API data; a store only for complex shared client state.

expectedPoints.label: Local state first, lift when needed
expectedPoints.description: Keep state in the component that uses it; lift to the nearest common ancestor only when shared.
acceptedSignals: keep it close to where it's used

expectedPoints.label: Server data is not client state
expectedPoints.description: API data belongs in a server-cache tool (React Query/SWR) with caching and invalidation, not in a global store.
acceptedSignals: don't put api data in redux

expectedPoints.label: Knows when a store earns its place
expectedPoints.description: Complex, widely shared, frequently changing client state (or context re-render limits) justifies Redux/Zustand.

followUps.prompt: What is the performance pitfall of putting a frequently-changing value into React context?

questions.title: SSR vs CSR — do you need Next.js?
questions.prompt: When would you choose server-side rendering over a plain client-side React app? What does hydration mean in this picture?
questions.answerStructureHint: SEO and first-paint needs vs infra complexity; hydration attaches interactivity to server HTML; CSR is fine behind login.

expectedPoints.label: SSR for SEO and first paint
expectedPoints.description: Public, content-heavy pages need indexable HTML and fast first contentful paint; SSR/SSG delivers that.

expectedPoints.label: Explains hydration
expectedPoints.description: Server HTML becomes interactive when React attaches listeners client-side; mismatch and cost are the catch.

expectedPoints.label: CSR is fine for app-like UIs
expectedPoints.description: Dashboards behind login don't need SSR; SSR adds server infra, complexity and cost.

followUps.prompt: How do React Server Components change this trade-off?

questions.title: Optimizing unnecessary re-renders
questions.prompt: A React page re-renders too much and feels slow. Walk me through how you approach optimizing it — and how you would render a list of one million items.

expectedPoints.label: Measure first, structural fixes before memo
expectedPoints.description: Profile; keep state local, split components, trim props — memoization is not the first move.

expectedPoints.label: React.memo / useMemo / useCallback
expectedPoints.description: Component skip, cached value, cached function — each defeats a specific re-render cause.

expectedPoints.label: Stable references or memo is defeated
expectedPoints.description: Fresh object/array/inline function props defeat React.memo.

expectedPoints.label: Virtualization for huge lists
expectedPoints.description: Pagination/infinite scroll/windowing — only visible rows mounted (react-window).

questions.title: Which state goes where?
questions.prompt: You join a React project where everything lives in one global store. How do you think about what state belongs where?

expectedPoints.label: State types → different tools
expectedPoints.description: Local UI / shared / complex app / server / navigation state each has a right home.

expectedPoints.label: Prop drilling threshold
expectedPoints.description: 1–2 levels of props is fine; many unrelated intermediaries → Context or store.

expectedPoints.label: Context+useReducer and its limits
expectedPoints.description: Lightweight Redux alternative; split by domain; no devtools/middleware/selectors.

expectedPoints.label: When Redux earns its place
expectedPoints.description: Cross-module events + predictable updates; selectors decouple store shape.

questions.title: Structuring a large React app
questions.prompt: How would you structure a large React application, and when do you decide a component should become reusable?

expectedPoints.label: Feature-based boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Routes are entry points; components/hooks/API/state live inside their feature; shared layer for generic UI.

expectedPoints.label: Reuse on repetition, not prediction
expectedPoints.description: Generalize when the pattern repeats; clear API, sensible defaults, no product logic; too many props → split.

expectedPoints.label: Designs: hunt the missing states
expectedPoints.description: Loading/error/empty/disabled/permissions, responsiveness, long localized text; clarify early.

questions.title: Migrating a jQuery UI to React
questions.prompt: The product has a large jQuery/server-template UI and the team wants React. How do you run that migration?

expectedPoints.label: Incremental, no big bang
expectedPoints.description: Isolated areas first, small reviewable steps, behaviour kept stable, strangler-style shrinking of legacy.

expectedPoints.label: Tests around critical flows first
expectedPoints.description: Safety net before touching risky paths; verify before and after.

expectedPoints.label: Refactor by risk/value, not style
expectedPoints.description: Actively changing or blocking code first; leave stable legacy alone.

expectedPoints.label: Old-React modernization details
expectedPoints.description: Dependency audit, deprecated APIs, createRoot; class components only as needed.

questions.title: Frontend quality and safe releases
questions.prompt: What does your quality toolchain look like on a frontend project, and how do you reduce the risk of a release going wrong?

expectedPoints.label: Layered quality tools
expectedPoints.description: Typecheck → lint/format → unit/component → E2E on critical flows → CI gates before merge.

expectedPoints.label: Regression + visual regression
expectedPoints.description: New changes must not break existing behaviour; screenshot comparison for the UI.

expectedPoints.label: Feature flags for release safety
expectedPoints.description: Deploy ≠ enable; gradual rollout; kill switch; watch for flag debt.

questions.title: Agreeing an API contract with the backend
questions.prompt: A new feature needs new endpoints. What do you clarify with the backend team before you start building the UI?

expectedPoints.label: Shapes and error format
expectedPoints.description: Request/response shapes plus error codes and error body — validation errors included.

expectedPoints.label: UI states and pagination
expectedPoints.description: Loading/empty/error states; offset vs cursor pagination.

expectedPoints.label: Auth semantics and compatibility
expectedPoints.description: 401 vs 403 handling; backward compatibility during migration.

expectedPoints.label: REST vs GraphQL judgment
expectedPoints.description: REST for resource CRUD; GraphQL when the client must control data shape / avoid over-fetching.

questions.title: How do you use AI in development?
questions.prompt: How do you use AI tools in your development workflow, and where do you draw the line?

expectedPoints.label: Accelerator use cases
expectedPoints.description: Boilerplate, refactoring, test generation, docs, exploring options.

expectedPoints.label: Engineer keeps responsibility
expectedPoints.description: Generated code is reviewed for edge cases, types, security, performance, maintainability.

expectedPoints.label: Guardrails named
expectedPoints.description: Tests, type checking, code review, architectural ownership make generated code safe.
